heading: PROEMIO 17
card_summary: This chunk concludes Zorzi's dedicatory Proem to Pope Clement VII and opens the first chapter of the first Canticle of De Harmonia Mundi. The Proem's closing pages present the work's tripartite structure — three Cantici of eight tones each, organized on the octave — with the first covering the harmony of both worlds and the Archetype, the second the Messiah's restoration of dissonance caused by sin, and the third humanity's recall to harmony with the Creator. Zorzi defends his use of Hebrew divine names by appealing to Origen's authority that divine power is lost in translation. The opening of Caput I then poses the question of who is the progenitor of the world, citing Plato's dictum that the Maker is as difficult to find as to speak of worthily, and invokes the Pythagorean requirement that no one speak of divine things without divine illumination (Psalm 36:9 "In lumine tuo videbimus lumen"). Zorzi proceeds through a sacred genealogy of this illuminated knowledge: Adam, Seth/Enos, Enoch, Noah (identified with the Latin Janus via Aramaic etymology), and Abraham — each receiving progressively brighter light and transmitting theological, cosmological, and ritual wisdom to surrounding peoples including the Chaldeans, Scythians, and Italians.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 42 spans two distinct but continuous textual moments: the closing paragraphs of Zorzi's dedicatory Proem to Pope Clement VII (concluding the long preamble begun earlier) and the opening of the first chapter of Caput I, which asks "Who is the progenitor of the world?" Together they establish both the formal architecture of the *De Harmonia Mundi* and its epistemological ground — the claim that genuine knowledge of God requires divine illumination and flows through a continuous chain of enlightened figures from Adam onward.

---

## The Structural Plan: Three Cantici on the Octave

Zorzi announces that the entire work will be organized into **three Cantici**, each comprising eight tones, forming a perfect octave. The structural logic mirrors the cosmological argument: the Creator himself organized creation through the octave, resting on the seventh day and completing the pattern in the eighth, which is simultaneously identical and non-identical with the first ("che coincide e nel contempo non coincide con il primo"). This recursive identity is the formal signature of harmony.

> Abbiamo creduto di poter abbracciare interamente questo accordo in tre cantici modulati sull'ottava, ciascun cantico sarà composto di otto toni, formando perciò un'ottava perfetta. Nel primo cantico faremo risuonare la consonanza reciproca tra entrambi i mondi e tra essi e l'Archetipo.

<cite>p. 185–186</cite>

The three Cantici have distinct objects:
1. **First Canticle**: the reciprocal consonance between the two worlds (celestial and terrestrial) and between both and the Archetype.
2. **Second Canticle**: the correspondence of all things with the person of the Messiah, and the restoration of dissonance (caused by sin) through Christ.
3. **Third Canticle**: man's recall to harmony with all things and with the Creator ("al quale tutto deve aderire, o meglio, essere unito").

The Latin passage (retained in the chunk) makes clear that in these three cantici is contained "totum utriusque mundi negotium, et disciplinarum multarum summa" — the entire business of both worlds and the sum of many disciplines.

---

## Defense of Hebrew Divine Names and Methodological Notes

Zorzi anticipates criticism of two procedural choices: (1) his use of original Hebrew (and Hebrew-proximate) forms of divine names in Latin, and (2) apparent repetitions in the text. He defends the first by appealing to **Origen**'s authority that divine names translated into another language lose their power entirely — a classical Christian Kabbalist position that preserves the efficacy of the Hebrew *shem* even within a Latin theological treatise. He insists that deploying "tali parole originarie, o a forme a esse assai prossime" does not make him superficial or bizarre, but rather faithful to the deepest significances concealed in the very form and letters of those names.

> affinché possiamo esprimere con la massima chiarezza i profondi significati che si celano nella forma delle lettere che li compongono, a tal punto da far ritenere a Origene che quelle verità, tradotte in un'altra lingua, non conservassero nulla della propria potenza.

<cite>p. 186</cite>

This defense of the *virtus nominum* in their original linguistic form is a key marker of Zorzi's debt to Kabbalistic name-theology (the doctrine that Hebrew divine names are not arbitrary conventions but ontologically efficacious).

---

## Dedication and Submission to Clement VII

The Proem closes with an elaborate dedicatory submission to Pope Clement VII. Zorzi acknowledges that novelty attracts envy from those "not accustomed to a new light" ("occhi miopi, non avvezzi a una luce nuova"), and that his work's combination of new method and smoldering "scintille" (sparks of hidden knowledge recently struck from the flint) will draw critics. He therefore submits the entire work to the Pope's censure. The dedication language is eucharistic and ecclesial: the work should be made "un cibo sano per quanti se ne nutrono" (wholesome food for those who feed on it), and the Pope, as vicar of Christ "in quo omnia, et per quem omnia," is the source through whom the work will live and be beautiful.

The Latin conclusion of the Proem reads:

> Nec existimaverim Pater Sancte aIVv et Clementissime Clemens hoc nostrum qualecumque opus vere concinnum, nisi tua virtute, et authoritate omnibus membris ecclesiastici corporis reddatur suave, et innocuus cibus sumentibus.

<cite>p. 187</cite>

---

## Opening of Caput I: Who Is the Progenitor of the World?

Caput I opens with the programmatic question: who is the world's progenitor, and with what harmony does he enjoy unity? Zorzi immediately cites **Plato** (Timaeus context) — "Opificem, genitoremque universi tam invenire difficile, quam inventum digne profari, cum lumen inhabitet inaccessibile" — that the Maker is as difficult to find as to speak of worthily, dwelling in inaccessible light. The Platonic and Pauline (1 Tim. 6:16) resonances merge here.

Against this apophatic difficulty, Zorzi invokes **Pythagoras**'s injunction that no one presume to speak of divine things without divine light, and grounds this light in the Psalmist: "In lumine tuo videbimus lumen" (Psalm 36:9). This becomes the epistemological axiom for the entire inquiry.

> Ideo iubet Pythagoras, ut de divinis nemo sine lumine loqui praesumat, sine illo inquam lumine, de quo psalmographus canit: In lumine tuo videbimus lumen.

<cite>p. 188</cite>

---

## The Genealogy of Illuminated Knowledge

Zorzi then traces a **sacred genealogy** of figures who received this divine illumination progressively, each transmitting cosmological, theological, and ritual knowledge:

- **Adam** (*protoplastes*): illuminated, saw God "walking at noon," recognized his voice, perceived the natures of all created things sufficiently to impose proper names — a reference to the Adamic language tradition.
- **Seth and Enos**: through Seth's begetting of Enos, the hidden name of the Lord began to be invoked ("coepit invocare nomen Domini adhuc occultum").
- **Enoch**: prophesied future things and was translated into God himself.
- **Noah**: identified with the Latin **Janus** (the vine-bearer) via the **Aramaic** etymology — "Noe siquidem Ianus (qui vitiferum signat) lingua Aramea dicitur." This cross-cultural identification is confirmed by appeal to **Berosus** (Chaldean and Scythian records). Noah not only predicted the flood but subsequently taught the Armenians, Scythians, and Italians (*Chitimi* in Scripture) theology, sacred rites, the secrets of nature, the courses of the stars, solar years, and lunar months.
- **Abraham**: received this light "quanto posterius, tanto fulgidius" — the later, the brighter — and taught better rites and the sign of election.

This genealogical schema serves Zorzi's prisca theologia framework: pagan wisdom (Greek philosophy, Pythagorean number theory, Orphic theology) ultimately derives from the same divine illumination that animated the Hebrew patriarchs. The list of "guides" at the opening of the chunk — Mercurio, Aglaofemo, Orfeo, Esiodo, Pitagora, Archita, Platone, Parmenide, Caronda, Zalmoxi, Licurgo — are all tributaries of this single Adamic-patriarchal spring.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

The structural announcement of three Cantici on the octave model crystallizes the cosmological architecture of the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*. The octave is not merely metaphor: it is the mathematical-musical structure through which God disposed creation (the six days plus Sabbath forming the seventh, with the eighth as eschatological return). Zorzi explicitly ties the book's own organization to this divine pattern — "seguendo le tracce dell'Autore" — so that the text enacts formally what it describes cosmologically. The defense of Hebrew names situates the work within the Christian Kabbalist tradition that runs from Pico della Mirandola through Johannes Reuchlin, while the genealogy of illuminated sages anchors it in the broader Renaissance *prisca theologia* synthesized by Ficino. The submission to Clement VII performs the orthodox ecclesial framing that distinguishes Zorzi's Kabbalah from suspect heterodoxy.
